title: Mortgage pain spreads to affluent suburbs
full_text:
Mortgage stress has spread beyond the outer suburban mortgage belt into more upmarket areas and the pressure is expected to increase as the housing market is hit by the federal budget’s backflip on property taxes.
The pain hitting mortgage-holders adds a new dimension to the difficulties facing the housing market where renters face the prospect of increases and bank economists say house prices could have their most dramatic fall in four decades.
Surprise changes to both negative gearing and the capital gains tax discount rules will alter the risks and rewards for property investors as they may no longer be able to carry the burden of loss-making investments, research house Digital Finance Analytics reports. Its latest mortgage stress report shows the burden on borrowers is already rising, including at the higher end of the market.
Research adviser Mansour Soltani said mortgage stress was notably higher than this time last year, particularly across middle-income, outer-suburban areas that had traditionally been viewed as relatively stable.
He cited the cocktail of pressures facing households: higher mortgage repayments, rising living costs, reduced savings buffers and growing uncertainty around property policy, that were hitting simultaneously.
“The proposed changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax matter because they change the risk-reward equation for property investors,” Mr Soltani said.
“For many years, investors were prepared to carry short-term losses because the expectation was that long-term capital growth, combined with tax incentives, would outweigh the holding costs.” He said if those incentives were reduced, some investors might be less willing to absorb ongoing cashflow pressure.
The impact has already been seen in plunging auction clearance rates in Sydney, which has a larger cohort of investment stock than other cities.
While there had been anecdotal reports of some sellers pulling their properties from the market, the preliminary auction clearance rate edged higher over the past week, from the post-budget shock a week ago. Cotality Australia reported a 58.2 per cent combined capitals clearance rate, up from the 57.5 per cent a week ago. This was revised down to 50.4 per cent on final numbers and some analysts said the market was on par with the initial days of the pandemic in 2020.
The DFA report identifies Sydney’s affluent lower north shore postcode, 2066, as Australia’s biggest severe mortgage stress hotspot in the first quarter. It covers suburbs including Lane Cove, Northwood, Riverview, Longueville and Linley Point. The number of severely stressed households in the area rose from 749 to 2306.
Brisbane and southeast Queensland were the fastest-growing mortgage default risk markets, with eight of the top 10- ranked postcodes posting increases in the quarter. The Gold Coast and Logan corridor showed particularly sharp deterioration, where postcode 4207 experienced a 73.3 per cent leap in mortgage default risk over the quarter.
Melbourne’s outer growth suburbs also deteriorated. The western-suburban postcode 3030 covering Point Cook, Werribee and Wyndham had a 51.4 per cent quarterly increase in mortgage stress. Meanwhile, 3199, Frankston and Karingal, had a 28 per cent surge in mortgage stress.
The pressures were biting ahead of the tax backflip which is expected to crimp property investors as they pull back from established property.
“We expect this could lead to more cautious investor behaviour, particularly around existing properties, and place additional pressure on borrowers who are already heavily leveraged,” Mr Soltani said.
The spillover will hit financial institutions which have already flagged a pullback, imposing stricter loan limits.
“Banks are already reassessing parts of their investor lending settings and serviceability models. Any reduction in investor confidence generally results in lenders becoming more conservative around risk,” Mr Soltani said.
The DFA report shows mortgage stress is no longer concentrated only in traditionally lower-income areas and is now entrenched in Middle Australia.
“We are increasingly seeing stress emerge across dual-income, middle-ring suburbs where borrowers took on large debts during the low-rate environment and are now adjusting to structurally higher repayment costs,” Mr Soltani said.
He said many households had already been under pressure before the government policy switch.
“In that environment, even relatively targeted tax changes can have an outsized impact on sentiment and borrower behaviour,” Mr Soltani said.
“At a market level, the government appears to be trying to slow speculative demand and reduce the risk of a larger housing correction later. The challenge will be balancing affordability objectives without significantly weakening investor participation at a time when housing supply remains constrained.” Renters are already in the firing line, despite the federal government claiming 75,000 extra first-home buyers will enter the market over a decade as a result of its policy.
Ray White chief economist Nerida Conisbee said rents would rise if the policies reduced the number of rental properties in high-demand suburbs. She said the government’s grandfathering of existing negatively geared homes acknowledged the risk of the rental pool shrinking.
Ms Conisbee said policies reducing investor participation could moderate price growth for buyers, but they could also tighten rental supply and put more pressure on renters. She said the policy change could result in fewer rental properties in high-demand, established suburbs for long-term renters, which would drive up their rents. They could also be unable to compete with other bidders to buy the homes.
